dominant_error_pattern: Escalating colleague misconduct directly to NASP's ethics board or to administration without first addressing the violation through collegial dialogue.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing A or B skip the restorative first step. Teach the escalation sequence: (1) direct conversation with Sean, (2) if unresolved, file a grievance. Option C (discuss then escalate if it continues) respects both the colleague relationship and the seriousness of the violation. Show why A (immediate NASP filing) and B (straight to admin) foreclose dialogue and burn bridges prematurely.

dominant_error_pattern: Treating all aversive techniques as equally inappropriate or assuming administrative endorsement makes aversive methods ethically sound for any behavioral situation.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing A or C misunderstand the safety exception. Teach: Aversive techniques are restricted except when necessary to prevent imminent harm. Option B (safety issues only) sets the ethical boundary. Show why A (never use) ignores life-threatening situations and C (if admin approves) lets policy override ethics. Option D (appropriate for elementary students) ignores developmental considerations entirely.

dominant_error_pattern: Responding to a student's disclosure about a family member's substance use with additional interviewing or parental contact instead of consulting with mandated reporter resources and legal authorities first.
instructional_red_flags: Students who pick A, B, or D skip the consultation step. Teach: Before acting, consult with social services or legal resources to understand your reporting obligations in this jurisdiction (uncle's residence matters; visit frequency matters). Option C (consult without names) maintains confidentiality while getting expert guidance on whether this triggers mandated reporting. Show why A (more questions) delays expert input, B (admin first) may not know legal requirements, and D (parent call) risks tipping off a potentially unsafe situation.
